info.title: Creating and analyzing a core dump

abstract.para: You can create and analyze a core dump using systemd-coredump

topic.para: The following procedure uses Vim to trigger a segmentation fault (segfault) for testing purposes.

step.para: Ensure the debuginfo-pool and debuginfo-update repositories are active for your registration.
Install the debug information for Vim.
To trigger a crash, open a file with Vim, then from another terminal, send a SIGSEGV signal to the process:
step.screen: ps ax | grep vim # kill -s SIGSEGV <PID>

step.para: Use coredumpctl; to verify the crash was captured:
An asterisk * in the PRESENT column indicates the dump file is available on disk.
To view the stack trace and metadata of the latest crash:
To load a specific crash (for example, PID 2345) directly into the debugger:

section.title: Managing Core Dump Storage
section.para: To minimize the storage of sensitive information, you can disable disk storage and log only to the journal by modifying /etc/systemd/coredump.conf

listitem.para: Storage=none Dumps are logged in the journal but the memory image is not saved to disk.
Storage=journal Cores are stored directly within the systemd journal.

section.para: Core dumps are not preserved after a system restart unless moved. To save a core dump to a permanent file:
